title: Advanced Agent Architectures — Planning, Memory, Multi-Agent & Production
description: Production-grade agent systems at FAANG Staff+ level require sophisticated planning, multi-agent coordination, memory architectures, sandboxed execution, and comprehensive observability. This guide builds on the ReAct/CoT foundations and covers the systems engineering of scalable, reliable agent deployments.

sections.title: Agent Planning: Beyond ReAct — LLMCompiler, Plan-Solve, DFSDT
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: ReAct's thought-action-observation loop is simple but inefficient — it plans one step at a time. Advanced planners decompose the entire task upfront and execute in parallel where possible. LLMCompiler (2024) generates a complete DAG of tasks, executes independent tasks in parallel, and dynamically replans when tasks fail. Plan-Solve first generates a high-level plan, then executes each step with detailed sub-plans. DFSDT (Depth-First Search with Decision Trees) explores multiple action paths and backtracks on failure. The Staff-level insight: the planning strategy determines the latency-accuracy Pareto frontier. Deeper planning = higher accuracy but higher latency and cost.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: LLMCompiler reduces agent loop latency by 1.5-3x compared to ReAct (parallel task execution). Plan-Solve improves accuracy by 5-15% on complex reasoning tasks (HotpotQA, GSM8K) compared to direct ReAct. DFSDT with depth=5 and beam=3 achieves 8-12% higher success rate than ReAct on web navigation tasks (WebArena). Cost: planning overhead adds 10-30% more tokens (the plan is prepended to the execution trace). The sweet spot: use LLMCompiler for latency-sensitive tasks, Plan-Solve for accuracy-critical tasks, DFSDT for tasks requiring exploration (debugging, web navigation).

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) For simple tasks (1-3 steps), use ReAct (lowest overhead). 2) For complex tasks with parallel subtasks, use LLMCompiler (generate DAG, execute in parallel). 3) For tasks needing verification, add a verify step after execution (check results, retry if wrong). 4) For exploration-heavy tasks, use DFSDT with backtracking. 5) Always set a planning depth limit (default 5) to bound token usage. 6) Monitor plan quality: how many planned steps succeed vs require replanning.
sections.codeBlock:
# LLMCompiler: Parallel task DAG execution
from typing import Any
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
import asyncio, json

@dataclass
class Task:
    id: str
    description: str
    dependencies: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)  # task IDs this depends on
    tool: str = ""
    inputs: dict = field(default_factory=dict)
    status: str = "pending"  # pending | running | done | failed
    result: Any = None


class LLMCompiler:
    """
    Decomposes a task into a DAG of parallel-executable subtasks.
    Independent tasks run concurrently, dependent tasks wait for inputs.
    """
    def __init__(self, llm, tools):
        self.llm = llm
        self.tools = tools

    def plan(self, task: str) -> list[Task]:
        """
        Ask LLM to decompose task into a DAG.
        Returns list of Task objects with dependency annotations.
        """
        prompt = f"""Decompose this task into parallel-executable subtasks.
For each subtask, specify:
- id: unique name
- description: what to do
- dependencies: list of subtask IDs that must complete first
- tool: which tool to use (or 'llm' for LLM reasoning)
- inputs: what inputs it needs (use ${{task_id.result}} for dependency outputs)

Task: {task}

Return as JSON list."""

        response = self.llm.generate(prompt)
        try:
            tasks_data = json.loads(response)
            return [Task(**t) for t in tasks_data]
        except:
            # Fallback to single task
            return [Task(id="main", description=task, tool="llm")]

    async def execute(self, task: str) -> str:
        """Plan -> Execute DAG -> Return result."""
        tasks = self.plan(task)
        results: dict[str, Any] = {}

        while True:
            # Find tasks whose dependencies are resolved
            ready = [t for t in tasks if t.status == "pending" and
                     all(dep in results for dep in t.dependencies)]

            if not ready:
                break

            # Execute ready tasks in parallel
            async def run_task(t: Task):
                t.status = "running"
                # Resolve dependency references in inputs
                resolved_inputs = {}
                for k, v in t.inputs.items():
                    if isinstance(v, str) and "${" in v:
                        for dep_id, dep_result in results.items():
                            v = v.replace(f"${{{dep_id}.result}}", str(dep_result))
                    resolved_inputs[k] = v

                if t.tool == "llm":
                    result = self.llm.generate(t.description)
                else:
                    result = await self.tools[t.tool](**resolved_inputs)

                t.status = "done"
                t.result = result
                results[t.id] = result
                return t

            await asyncio.gather(*[run_task(t) for t in ready])

        # Collect final result
        final = results.get("final", list(results.values())[-1] if results else "")
        return str(final)


# === Plan-and-Solve ===
class PlanSolve:
    """
    Two-phase planning: generate plan -> execute each step.
    More structured than ReAct, less parallel than LLMCompiler.
    """
    def __init__(self, llm, tools):
        self.llm = llm
        self.tools = tools

    async def run(self, task: str) -> str:
        # Phase 1: Generate plan
        plan_prompt = f"""Generate a high-level step-by-step plan for:
{task}

Each step should be achievable with available tools.
Available tools: {list(self.tools.keys())}

Return as numbered list."""
        plan = self.llm.generate(plan_prompt)

        # Phase 2: Execute each step
        steps = [s.strip() for s in plan.split("\n") if s.strip() and s[0].isdigit()]
        results = []
        for i, step in enumerate(steps):
            execute_prompt = f"""Task: {task}
Plan step {i+1}: {step}
Previous results: {results}

Execute this step. Use tools if needed."""
            result = self.llm.generate(execute_prompt)
            results.append(result)

        # Phase 3: Summarize
        summary_prompt = f"""Task: {task}
All step results: {results}

Provide the final answer."""
        return self.llm.generate(summary_prompt)

sections.title: Agent Memory Systems: Short-Term, Long-Term, Episodic & Semantic
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Agents need four memory types. Short-term memory is the in-context conversation history (limited by context window). Long-term memory stores factual knowledge retrieved via embeddings (vector DB). Episodic memory stores past experiences (task trajectories, successes, failures) — Reflexion-style reflections are episodic. Semantic memory stores learned skills and patterns (how to use tools, common workflows). The key Staff-level insight: memory is the primary differentiator between toy agents and production agents. An agent without long-term or episodic memory repeats the same mistakes and forgets what it learned.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Short-term memory: context window of 8K-200K tokens depending on model. Long-term memory: vector DB with embeddings (1536d, cosine similarity) — retrieval recall at top-5 >85%. Episodic memory: Reflexion-style reflections improve task success rate by 20-40% on programming tasks after 5-10 episodes. Semantic memory: skill libraries reduce task completion time by 30-50% (skills are pre-optimized patterns). Memory retrieval latency: short-term (0ms, in context), long-term (50-200ms, vector DB query), episodic (100-500ms, reflection retrieval + ranking).

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Use short-term memory for current conversation context (always). 2) Implement long-term memory via embedding + vector DB for factual knowledge. 3) Implement episodic memory: after each task, generate a reflection (what worked, what didn't), store with the task embedding. 4) At the start of a new task, retrieve relevant episodic memories and prepend them to the prompt. 5) Implement memory consolidation: periodically summarize episodic memories into semantic memory (skill patterns). 6) Set memory limits: max 50 long-term retrievals per task, max 10 episodic retrievals per task.
sections.codeBlock:
# Agent memory architecture
from typing import Any
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
import json, time, numpy as np

@dataclass
class EpisodicMemory:
    task_description: str
    trajectory: list[str]  # steps taken
    success: bool
    reflection: str  # what worked/what didn't
    embedding: list[float] = field(default_factory=list)
    timestamp: float = field(default_factory=time.time)


@dataclass
class LongTermMemory:
    key: str
    content: str
    metadata: dict
    embedding: list[float] = field(default_factory=list)


class AgentMemory:
    """
    Unified memory system with short-term, long-term, episodic, and semantic stores.
    """
    def __init__(self, embed_model):
        self.embed = embed_model
        self.short_term: list[dict] = []  # in-context messages
        self.long_term: list[LongTermMemory] = []  # vector store
        self.episodic: list[EpisodicMemory] = []  # experience store
        self.semantic: dict[str, str] = {}  # learned patterns

    def add_to_short_term(self, role: str, content: str):
        self.short_term.append({"role": role, "content": content})
        # Trim to context window limit
        if len(self.short_term) > 100:  # arbitrary limit
            self.short_term = self.short_term[-50:]  # keep recent half

    def add_long_term(self, key: str, content: str, metadata: dict = None):
        embedding = self._embed(content)
        self.long_term.append(LongTermMemory(
            key=key, content=content, metadata=metadata or {},
            embedding=embedding
        ))

    def search_long_term(self, query: str, top_k: int = 5) -> list[str]:
        """Retrieve relevant long-term memories."""
        query_emb = self._embed(query)
        if not self.long_term:
            return []
        # Cosine similarity
        embeddings = np.array([m.embedding for m in self.long_term])
        scores = np.dot(embeddings, query_emb)
        top_indices = np.argsort(scores)[-top_k:][::-1]
        return [self.long_term[i].content for i in top_indices]

    def record_episode(self, task: str, trajectory: list[str], success: bool) -> str:
        """Record an experience and generate reflection."""
        reflection = self._generate_reflection(task, trajectory, success)
        memory = EpisodicMemory(
            task_description=task,
            trajectory=trajectory,
            success=success,
            reflection=reflection,
            embedding=self._embed(task)
        )
        self.episodic.append(memory)
        return reflection

    def retrieve_episodes(self, task: str, top_k: int = 3) -> list[str]:
        """Retrieve relevant past experiences for a new task."""
        task_emb = self._embed(task)
        if not self.episodic:
            return []
        embeddings = np.array([e.embedding for e in self.episodic])
        scores = np.dot(embeddings, task_emb)
        top_indices = np.argsort(scores)[-top_k:][::-1]
        return [self.episodic[i].reflection for i in top_indices]

    def learn_semantic(self, pattern_name: str, pattern: str):
        """Store a learned semantic pattern (skill)."""
        self.semantic[pattern_name] = pattern

    def _generate_reflection(self, task: str, trajectory: list[str], success: bool) -> str:
        """Generate a structured reflection."""
        # In production, use an LLM call here
        status = "succeeded" if success else "failed"
        return f"Task '{task}' {status}. Key steps: {' -> '.join(trajectory[-3:])}"

    def _embed(self, text: str) -> list[float]:
        return self.embed(text)

    def get_context(self, task: str) -> str:
        """Build enriched context from all memory stores."""
        relevant_knowledge = self.search_long_term(task, top_k=3)
        relevant_episodes = self.retrieve_episodes(task, top_k=2)
        parts = []
        if relevant_knowledge:
            parts.append("Knowledge:\n" + "\n".join(relevant_knowledge))
        if relevant_episodes:
            parts.append("Past experiences:\n" + "\n".join(relevant_episodes))
        if self.semantic:
            parts.append("Patterns:\n" + json.dumps(self.semantic))
        return "\n\n".join(parts)

sections.title: Multi-Agent Frameworks: LangGraph, CrewAI & AutoGen Patterns
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Multi-agent systems distribute intelligence across specialized agents. Three dominant frameworks: LangGraph models agent workflows as state graphs — nodes are agents or tools, edges are conditional transitions. CrewAI uses role-based agents (researcher, writer, reviewer) with defined tasks and processes (sequential, hierarchical, or consensual). AutoGen from Microsoft uses conversable agents that exchange messages through a structured chat loop. The Staff-level choice depends on the coordination pattern needed: LangGraph for complex state machines (need deterministic control flow), CrewAI for role-based teams (predictable agent interactions), AutoGen for emergent, chat-based coordination (agents negotiate through conversation).

bar_chart Concrete numbers: LangGraph supports state graphs with up to 100+ nodes, checkpoints for fault tolerance, and human-in-the-loop breakpoints. CrewAI manages 3-10 agents per crew, with task delegation overhead of 10-20% (agent communication). AutoGen group chat with 5 agents converges in 3-7 rounds for most tasks (vs 8-15 for unconstrained multi-agent). At FAANG scale: production multi-agent systems use 2-5 agents (more than 5 rarely improves quality). The most common pattern: supervisor + 2-4 specialist workers.

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Start with a single agent with specialized tools (avoid multi-agent unless necessary). 2) If single-agent fails due to context overflow or conflicting responsibilities, split into 2-3 agents. 3) Define clear agent roles and responsibilities (system prompts). 4) Implement a supervisor agent that routes tasks and resolves conflicts. 5) Set max rounds of agent communication (default 5) to bound cost. 6) Use LangGraph for deterministic workflows, CrewAI for role-based teams, AutoGen for emergent coordination. 7) Always checkpoint agent state after each step for fault tolerance.
sections.codeBlock:
# LangGraph: State machine for agent workflows
# from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
from typing import Any, TypedDict

# === LangGraph pattern: state graph with conditional edges ===
class AgentState(TypedDict):
    messages: list[dict]
    next_agent: str
    task: str
    result: str
    iteration_count: int


def create_agent_workflow():
    """
    Builds a LangGraph-style agent state machine.
    Nodes: router, researcher, writer, reviewer.
    Edges: conditional transitions based on state.
    """
    # In production with actual LangGraph:
    # workflow = StateGraph(AgentState)
    #
    # workflow.add_node("router", router_agent)
    # workflow.add_node("researcher", researcher_agent)
    # workflow.add_node("writer", writer_agent)
    # workflow.add_node("reviewer", reviewer_agent)

    # workflow.add_conditional_edges(
    #     "router",

    #     "reviewer",

    #                   else "finalize",
    #     {"accept": "writer", "finalize": END}
    # )

    # return workflow.compile()
    pass


# === CrewAI pattern: Role-based agents ===
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class CrewAgent:
    role: str
    goal: str
    backstory: str
    tools: list[str]


class CrewAI:
    """
    Simplified CrewAI pattern: role-based team with supervisor.
    """
    def __init__(self, llm):
        self.llm = llm
        self.agents: dict[str, CrewAgent] = {}
        self.tasks: dict[str, str] = {}  # agent_role -> task

    def add_agent(self, agent: CrewAgent):
        self.agents[agent.role] = agent

    def assign_task(self, role: str, task: str):
        self.tasks[role] = task

    async def run_sequential(self) -> dict[str, str]:
        """Sequential handoff: agent1 -> agent2 -> agent3"""
        results = {}
        for role, task in self.tasks.items():
            agent = self.agents[role]
            prompt = f"""Role: {agent.role}
Goal: {agent.goal}
Background: {agent.backstory}
Tools: {agent.tools}

Previous results: {results}

Your task: {task}

Provide your output."""
            result = self.llm.generate(prompt)
            results[role] = result
        return results


# === AutoGen pattern: Conversable agents ===
class ConversableAgent:
    """
    Simplified AutoGen pattern: agents chat until consensus.
    """
    def __init__(self, name: str, system_prompt: str, llm):
        self.name = name
        self.system = system_prompt
        self.llm = llm

    async def respond(self, context: list[dict]) -> str:
        messages = [{"role": "system", "content": self.system}] + context
        return self.llm.generate(messages)


class GroupChat:
    """Multi-agent chat with round-robin turn taking."""
    def __init__(self, agents: list[ConversableAgent], max_rounds: int = 5):
        self.agents = agents
        self.max_rounds = max_rounds
        self.history: list[dict] = []

    async def chat(self, task: str) -> str:
        self.history.append({"role": "user", "content": task})
        for round_num in range(self.max_rounds):
            for agent in self.agents:
                response = await agent.respond(self.history)
                self.history.append({"role": agent.name, "content": response})
                # Check for consensus
                if "AGREED" in response or "FINAL:" in response:
                    return response
        # Max rounds reached, summarize
        summary_prompt = f"Based on this discussion, provide a final answer:\n{self.history}"
        return self.agents[0].llm.generate(summary_prompt)

sections.title: Agent Sandboxing & Security
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Agents are inherently dangerous — they execute LLM-generated code, call external APIs, access data, and run autonomously. A compromised agent can exfiltrate data, delete resources, or execute arbitrary code. Sandboxing isolates each agent execution in a restricted environment: minimal Linux container, no network (or restricted network), read-only filesystem (except designated write paths), resource limits (CPU, memory, disk, runtime), and mandatory audit logging. The Staff-level insight: agents should run in a sandbox by default, with explicit escalation for privileged operations.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: gVisor (Google) or Firecracker (AWS) micro-VMs add ~50-200ms startup latency per agent invocation. Containers (Docker) add ~100-500ms startup. For latency-sensitive agents, reuse sandbox instances with a pool (warm start: <10ms). Memory overhead: ~50MB per sandbox (container) to ~200MB (micro-VM). At 1000 concurrent agents: 50GB+ memory overhead. FAANG uses a tiered approach: Tier 1 (no sandbox, trusted internal tools, <10ms overhead), Tier 2 (container sandbox, network restricted, <100ms), Tier 3 (micro-VM, full isolation, <500ms, for untrusted code execution).

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Classify agent tasks into trust tiers. 2) Tier 1 (read-only data, internal tools) → no sandbox. 3) Tier 2 (write operations, external APIs) → container sandbox. 4) Tier 3 (code execution, sensitive data) → micro-VM sandbox. 5) Use a sandbox pool for warm starts. 6) Enforce: max execution time (30s default), max memory (500MB), max network connections (per-IP rate limits). 7) Log all sandbox events (start, stop, network access, file access, exec calls). 8) Implement a kill switch: if an agent behaves anomalously (too many tool calls, unusual network destinations), terminate immediately.
sections.codeBlock:
# Agent sandbox infrastructure
from typing import Any
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
import asyncio, time

@dataclass
class SandboxConfig:
    level: str  # "none" | "container" | "microvm"
    max_cpu_cores: float = 1.0
    max_memory_mb: int = 500
    max_execution_s: float = 30.0
    allow_network: bool = False
    allowed_domains: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    read_only_paths: list[str] = field(default_factory=lambda: ["/data", "/config"])
    writable_paths: list[str] = field(default_factory=lambda: ["/tmp"])


class AgentSandboxPool:
    """
    Pool of pre-warmed sandboxes for low-latency agent execution.
    """
    def __init__(self, pool_size: int = 20):
        self.pool = asyncio.Queue(maxsize=pool_size)
        self._warm_pool(pool_size)

    def _warm_pool(self, size: int):
        for _ in range(size):
            self.pool.put_nowait(self._create_sandbox())

    def _create_sandbox(self) -> str:
        """Create a sandbox (container/micro-VM). Return sandbox ID."""
        # In production: Docker container or Firecracker micro-VM
        # sandbox_id = subprocess.run(["docker", "run", "-d", "agent-sandbox-image"])
        return "sandbox-" + str(id(self))

    async def acquire(self) -> str:
        """Get a warm sandbox from the pool."""
        return await self.pool.get()

    def release(self, sandbox_id: str):
        """Return sandbox to pool."""
        self.pool.put_nowait(sandbox_id)


class AgentExecutionGuard:
    """
    Monitors agent behavior and terminates anomalous activity.
    """
    def __init__(self):
        self.metrics: dict[str, dict] = {}

    def check(self, agent_id: str, action: str) -> bool:
        """Check if action is allowed. Returns True if OK, False if should terminate."""
        now = time.time()
        if agent_id not in self.metrics:
            self.metrics[agent_id] = {
                "tool_call_count": 0,
                "start_time": now,
                "network_destinations": set(),
                "file_access_count": 0
            }

        m = self.metrics[agent_id]
        m["tool_call_count"] += 1

        # Anomaly detection rules
        if m["tool_call_count"] > 50:  # too many tool calls
            return False
        if now - m["start_time"] > 60:  # running too long
            return False

        return True


# Production deployment diagram:
#
# User Request
#     |
# Agent Router (determines trust tier)
#     |
#     +-- Tier 1 (trusted): Direct execution, no sandbox
#     |       return result
#     |
#     +-- Tier 2 (semi-trusted): Container sandbox
#     |       acquire from pool -> execute -> release
#     |       return result
#     |
#     +-- Tier 3 (untrusted): Micro-VM sandbox
#             acquire micro-VM -> execute with network restricted
#             -> audit all actions -> destroy VM
#             return result + audit log

sections.title: Production Agent Infrastructure: Queuing, Checkpointing & Observability
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: A production agent system is a distributed system. An agent invocation goes through: task queue (Redis/SQS) for buffering and load leveling, worker pool (Kubernetes pods) for execution, state store (PostgreSQL/DynamoDB) for checkpointing after each step, timeout handler for stuck agents, and observability pipeline for monitoring. Each agent step is persisted — if a worker crashes mid-execution, another worker resumes from the last checkpoint. The Staff-level insight: agent systems fail in unpredictable ways (model hallucinations, tool timeouts, infinite loops). The infrastructure must handle all failure modes gracefully.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Task queue latency through Redis: <1ms. Checkpoint write: 10-50ms (DynamoDB). Worker cold start: 2-5s (Kubernetes). Agent step duration: 2-10s (median), 30s+ (p99). Typical agent: 4-7 steps. Total agent execution: 10-60s. At 1000 agents/day: ~100-1000 concurrent steps. Fault tolerance: 99.9% of crashed agents resume from checkpoint (the remaining 0.1% are unrecoverable — network partition during state write). Recovery time: 2-10s (detect crash + spin up new worker + load checkpoint).

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Enqueue agent tasks with a task queue (Redis for low latency, SQS for durability). 2) Workers pull tasks, execute agent steps, checkpoint after each step. 3) Implement timeout: if a step takes >30s, mark as failed and checkpoint with error. 4) Implement retry with backoff: 1s, 2s, 4s, max 3 retries per step. 5) Log each step: input, output, latency, tool calls, token usage. 6) Expose metrics: active agents, step duration p50/p95/p99, error rate, replay rate. 7) Implement a dashboard for live agent debugging (view agent state, replay steps, inject human feedback).
sections.codeBlock:
# Production agent worker with checkpointing
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Any
import asyncio, json, time, uuid
from enum import Enum

class StepStatus(Enum):
    PENDING = "pending"
    RUNNING = "running"
    COMPLETED = "completed"
    FAILED = "failed"
    TIMEOUT = "timeout"


@dataclass
class AgentStep:
    session_id: str
    step_number: int
    input: str = ""
    output: str = ""
    tool_calls: list[dict] = field(default_factory=list)
    status: StepStatus = StepStatus.PENDING
    started_at: float = 0.0
    completed_at: float = 0.0
    duration_ms: float = 0.0
    tokens_used: int = 0
    error: str = ""


class StateStore:
    """
    Persists agent state for fault tolerance and observability.
    """
    def __init__(self):
        self.sessions: dict[str, list[AgentStep]] = {}

    async def checkpoint(self, session_id: str, step: AgentStep):
        """Save step state. Idempotent (safe to retry)."""
        if session_id not in self.sessions:
            self.sessions[session_id] = []
        # Replace existing step at this number, or append
        existing = [s for s in self.sessions[session_id]
                    if s.step_number == step.step_number]
        if existing:
            self.sessions[session_id].remove(existing[0])
        self.sessions[session_id].append(step)

    async def load(self, session_id: str) -> list[AgentStep]:
        """Load all steps for a session (for resume after crash)."""
        return self.sessions.get(session_id, [])

    async def get_live_session(self, session_id: str) -> dict:
        """Get current agent state for dashboard."""
        steps = await self.load(session_id)
        return {
            "session_id": session_id,
            "step_count": len(steps),
            "current_step": steps[-1].step_number if steps else 0,
            "status": steps[-1].status.value if steps else "unknown",
            "duration_ms": sum(s.duration_ms for s in steps),
            "total_tokens": sum(s.tokens_used for s in steps)
        }


class AgentWorker:
    """
    Production agent worker with checkpointing, timeout, and observability.
    """
    def __init__(self, llm, tools, state_store: StateStore,
                 max_steps: int = 10, step_timeout: float = 30.0):
        self.llm = llm
        self.tools = tools
        self.state = state_store
        self.max_steps = max_steps
        self.step_timeout = step_timeout

    async def execute(self, task: str, session_id: str = None) -> str:
        """Execute agent task with checkpointing."""
        session_id = session_id or str(uuid.uuid4())

        # Resume from checkpoint if available
        existing_steps = await self.state.load(session_id)
        completed_steps = [s for s in existing_steps
                          if s.status in (StepStatus.COMPLETED, StepStatus.FAILED)]

        step_number = len(completed_steps) + 1
        context = json.dumps([s.__dict__ for s in completed_steps])

        while step_number <= self.max_steps:
            step = AgentStep(
                session_id=session_id,
                step_number=step_number,
                status=StepStatus.RUNNING,
                started_at=time.time()
            )

            try:
                # LLM call with timeout
                response = await asyncio.wait_for(
                    self.llm.chat([{"role": "user", "content": f"Context: {context}\n\nTask: {task}"}]),
                    timeout=self.step_timeout
                )

                step.output = response
                step.status = StepStatus.COMPLETED
                step.completed_at = time.time()
                step.duration_ms = (step.completed_at - step.started_at) * 1000

                await self.state.checkpoint(session_id, step)

                # Check if task is complete (model outputs FINAL)
                if "FINAL:" in response or "DONE" in response:
                    return response

            except asyncio.TimeoutError:
                step.status = StepStatus.TIMEOUT
                step.error = f"Step exceeded {self.step_timeout}s timeout"
                await self.state.checkpoint(session_id, step)
                break
            except Exception as e:
                step.status = StepStatus.FAILED
                step.error = str(e)
                await self.state.checkpoint(session_id, step)
                if step_number > 3:  # Allow retries for first 3 steps
                    break

            step_number += 1

        return f"Agent completed {step_number - 1}/{self.max_steps} steps."


# Staff insight: checkpointing enables powerful debugging.
# You can replay an agent step-by-step, inject a different tool result,
# and see how the agent reacts. This is the killer feature
# for production agent debugging.

sections.title: Agent Evaluation: Benchmarks, Metrics & Regression Testing
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Evaluating agents is fundamentally different from evaluating models. An agent's performance depends on planning quality, tool use accuracy, error recovery, and cost efficiency — not just answer correctness. The evaluation framework has four dimensions: task success (did the agent complete the goal?), efficiency (how many steps/tokens/cost?), robustness (does it handle errors, ambiguous input, tool failures?), and safety (does it follow constraints, avoid harmful actions?). At Staff+ level, you design eval suites that catch regressions before they reach production.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: AgentBench (2024) evaluates agents across 8 environments: OS, DB, web, reasoning, etc. LLM-as-judge evaluation correlates with human judgment at 0.8+ Cohen's kappa for task completion. Minimum eval set size: 200 tasks per scenario for statistically significant results. Cost: evaluating one agent on 200 tasks costs ~$50-200 in LLM API calls. Regression test suite: 20-50 tasks that must pass on every deployment (runs in <5 min, costs <$10). Production monitoring: track task completion rate (target >85%), average steps (target <8), tool error rate (target <5%), cost per task (budget per task type).

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Create a golden dataset of 200 tasks with expected outcomes (step sequences + final answer). 2) Run agents on the dataset weekly. 3) Measure: task completion rate, average steps, average tokens, cost per task, tool error rate. 4) Use LLM-as-judge to evaluate answer quality (scale 1-5). 5) Set regression gates: no more than 3% drop in task completion, no more than 5% increase in cost per task. 6) Track per-tool metrics: which tools are called most, which fail most, which add the most latency. 7) Continuously add failure cases to the eval set (when an agent fails in production, add that case to the eval).
sections.codeBlock:
# Agent evaluation framework
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Any
import json

@dataclass
class EvalTask:
    id: str
    instruction: str
    expected_steps: list[str]  # expected tool calls / milestones
    expected_answer: str
    tags: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)


@dataclass
class EvalResult:
    task_id: str
    success: bool
    steps_taken: int
    tokens_used: int
    cost: float
    tool_errors: int
    answer_quality: float  # 0-1, from LLM judge
    duration_ms: float


class AgentEvaluator:
    """
    Comprehensive agent evaluation suite.
    """
    def __init__(self, eval_tasks: list[EvalTask], llm_judge):
        self.tasks = eval_tasks
        self.judge = llm_judge
        self.results: list[EvalResult] = []

    async def evaluate(self, agent) -> dict:
        """Run all eval tasks and return aggregated metrics."""
        self.results = []
        for task in self.tasks:
            import time
            start = time.time()
            try:
                response = await agent.execute(task.instruction)
                duration = (time.time() - start) * 1000

                # Check success
                success = self._check_success(response, task)

                # LLM-as-judge for answer quality
                quality = await self._judge_quality(task, response)

                self.results.append(EvalResult(
                    task_id=task.id,
                    success=success,
                    steps_taken=len(response.split("\n")),
                    tokens_used=len(response.split()),  # rough
                    cost=len(response.split()) * 0.00003,  # rough $0.03/1K tokens
                    tool_errors=response.count("Error"),
                    answer_quality=quality,
                    duration_ms=duration
                ))
            except Exception as e:
                self.results.append(EvalResult(
                    task_id=task.id, success=False,
                    steps_taken=0, tokens_used=0, cost=0,
                    tool_errors=1, answer_quality=0.0,
                    duration_ms=(time.time() - start) * 1000
                ))

        return self._aggregate()

    def _check_success(self, response: str, task: EvalTask) -> bool:
        """Check if agent response matches expected answer."""
        return task.expected_answer.lower() in response.lower()

    async def _judge_quality(self, task: EvalTask, response: str) -> float:
        """Use LLM to judge answer quality."""
        judge_prompt = f"""
Task: {task.instruction}
Expected answer: {task.expected_answer}
Agent response: {response}

Rate the response quality from 0.0 (completely wrong) to 1.0 (perfect).
Output only the number."""
        result = await self.judge(judge_prompt)
        try:
            return min(1.0, max(0.0, float(result.strip())))
        except:
            return 0.5

    def _aggregate(self) -> dict:
        """Compute aggregate metrics."""
        if not self.results:
            return {}
        n = len(self.results)
        success_rate = sum(1 for r in self.results if r.success) / n
        avg_steps = sum(r.steps_taken for r in self.results) / n
        avg_cost = sum(r.cost for r in self.results) / n
        avg_quality = sum(r.answer_quality for r in self.results) / n
        avg_duration = sum(r.duration_ms for r in self.results) / n
        tool_error_rate = sum(r.tool_errors for r in self.results) / max(sum(r.steps_taken for r in self.results), 1)

        return {
            "task_count": n,
            "success_rate": round(success_rate, 3),
            "avg_steps": round(avg_steps, 1),
            "avg_cost": round(avg_cost, 4),
            "avg_quality": round(avg_quality, 3),
            "avg_duration_ms": round(avg_duration, 1),
            "tool_error_rate": round(tool_error_rate, 3),
            "pass": success_rate > 0.85
        }

sections.title: FAANG Q&A — Advanced Agent Architectures
sections.description:
search Q1 (Google L7): "Design a multi-agent system for incident response that handles 100+ alerts/day." A: Three-tier architecture. Tier 1: a classifier agent categorizes the alert (latency <1s). Tier 2: specialist agents (networking, database, application) investigate in parallel (latency 10-30s). Tier 3: a coordinator agent aggregates findings, determines severity, and drafts the response (latency 5-10s). If the coordinator can't determine the root cause, it escalates to a human operator with a summary of all findings. Each agent uses a shared state store for context sharing. The system achieves 70% auto-resolution rate within 60s.

search Q2 (Meta E6): "How do you prevent agents from entering infinite loops?" A: Four layers of defense. (1) Hard iteration limit (max 10 steps per task). (2) Stuck detection: if the same action repeats 3 times, force-terminate. (3) Diminishing returns: if no progress after 5 steps (measured by state change), switch to a simpler strategy. (4) Human-in-the-loop: after 7 steps without completion, pause and ask the user for guidance. Additionally, monitor for loop patterns (Repeated: Action A → Observation → Action A) and inject proactive breaking.

search Q3 (Anthropic L6): "Design a system that lets an agent reliably use 100+ tools without confusion." A: Hierarchical tool discovery. Top level: 10 category tools (search, database, communication, analytics, etc.) — each category is a virtual tool. When the agent calls a category tool, the system presents the top 10 tools in that category (ranked by relevance to the current context). This keeps the decision tree to depth 2 with 10 options each = 100 tools effectively. Combined with tool embeddings for relevance ranking. The agent never sees all 100 tools at once.

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models (Yao et al., 2022) — The foundational ReAct agent pattern.
  • LLMCompiler: An LLM Compiler for Parallel Function Calling (Kim et al., 2024) — DAG-based parallel agent execution. Dramatically reduces latency.
  • Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning (Shinn et al., 2023) — Episodic memory for agents.
  • LangGraph Documentation — State graph framework for agent workflows.
  • CrewAI Documentation — Role-based multi-agent framework.
  • AutoGen: Conversable Multi-Agent Framework (Microsoft) — Chat-based multi-agent coordination.
  • AgentBench: Evaluating LLMs as Agents — Comprehensive agent evaluation benchmark across 8 environments.

details:
Advanced Agent Decision Flow: Start with ReAct for simple agents. Upgrade to LLMCompiler for latency-sensitive parallel tasks. Add memory (long-term + episodic) for agents that learn from experience. Use LangGraph for deterministic state machine workflows, CrewAI for role-based teams, AutoGen for emergent chat-based coordination. Always sandbox agents (tiered by risk). Implement checkpointing for fault tolerance. Evaluate agents with task completion, efficiency, and robustness metrics. The difference between a demo agent and a production agent is checkpointing, sandboxing, observability, and evaluation.

Related: LLM Agents — ReAct, Tool Use & Reasoning (foundations) | Tool Use & Function Calling | AI Skills Architecture | MCP — Model Context Protocol
